questions.q: What is random access in an array?

questions.a: They are contiguous in memory, so sequential reads exploit spatial locality.

questions.q: When would you choose an array over a linked list?
questions.a: When you need fast indexing, compact storage, and mostly read-heavy access.

questions.q: What is the amortized cost of appending to a dynamic array?
Why can a resize be O(n) but appends still stay amortized O(1)?
questions.a: A resize copies all elements, but it happens rarely enough that the copy cost is spread across many cheap appends.

questions.q: When is a dynamic array a bad fit?
questions.a: When frequent inserts or deletes in the middle dominate and shifting elements becomes expensive.

questions.q: What is the search complexity of a singly linked list?
Why is insertion after a known node O(1)?
questions.a: Only the local next pointers change; no shifting of elements is needed.

questions.q: When is a singly linked list better than an array?
questions.a: When you already have the node and you expect frequent insertions or deletions near it.

questions.q: What extra pointer does a doubly linked list add?
Why does a doubly linked list make deletion easier?
questions.a: You can unlink a known node in O(1) without searching for its predecessor.

questions.q: When would you choose a doubly linked list over a singly linked list?
questions.a: When you need backward traversal or constant-time deletion from arbitrary positions.

questions.q: What access order does a stack enforce?
Why does a stack map well to recursion and backtracking?
questions.a: The most recent call or state is the next one that must be undone or explored.

questions.q: When should you choose a stack over a queue?
questions.a: When the newest work item must be handled first, such as DFS or undo/redo flows.

questions.q: What access order does a queue enforce?
Why is a circular buffer a good queue implementation?
questions.a: It avoids O(n) shifts on dequeue while keeping enqueue and dequeue O(1).

questions.q: When would you prefer a deque or circular buffer over a linked list queue?
questions.a: When you want O(1) ends with better cache behavior and predictable memory use.
